name: Multiple Sclerosis
signs:
Optic neuritis (vision loss, pain with eye movement)
Sensory deficits (numbness, tingling)
Lhermitte sign (electric sensation down spine with neck flexion)
Spasticity
Ataxia
Bladder dysfunction
Fatigue
Internuclear ophthalmoplegia
Uhthoff phenomenon (worsening with heat)
treatment:
1. Acute relapse: IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x3-5 days
2. Plasmapheresis for steroid-refractory relapses
3. Disease-modifying therapy (DMT) to reduce relapse rate
4. Symptomatic management (spasticity, pain, fatigue, bladder)
5. Physical therapy and rehabilitation
6. Vitamin D supplementation
meds:
Methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3-5 days (relapse)
Ocrelizumab 600mg IV q6 months
Natalizumab 300mg IV q4 weeks
Dimethyl fumarate 240mg PO BID
Fingolimod 0.5mg PO daily
Baclofen 5-20mg PO TID (spasticity)
Modafinil 200mg daily (fatigue)
Oxybutynin 5mg BID (bladder)
ddx: Neuromyelitis optica (NMOSD), CNS vasculitis, Sarcoidosis, SLE cerebritis, B12 deficiency, Lyme disease, HIV, ADEM
labs:
Brain MRI (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord T2/FLAIR lesions)
CSF: oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index
Visual evoked potentials (delayed)
Anti-AQP4 (rule out NMOSD)
Anti-MOG antibodies
Vitamin B12, RPR, HIV, Lyme
Revised McDonald criteria for diagnosis
notes:
McDonald criteria: dissemination in space AND time (can be satisfied by MRI alone)
Subtypes: relapsing-remitting (85%), secondary progressive, primary progressive
Prognostic factors: male sex, older onset, motor/cerebellar symptoms, frequent early relapses
JC virus screening (PML risk with natalizumab)
Pregnancy: reduced relapse rate, increase postpartum
description: A chronic autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the protective covering (myelin) of nerve fibers in the brain and spinal cord, causing communication problems between the brain and body.

name: Parkinson's Disease
signs:
Resting tremor (pill-rolling, 4-6 Hz)
Bradykinesia
Rigidity (cogwheel)
Postural instability (late feature)
Mask-like facies
Shuffling gait
Micrographia
Hypophonia
REM sleep behavior disorder
Anosmia
treatment:
1. Carbidopa/levodopa is most effective therapy
2. Dopamine agonists for younger patients (delay levodopa)
3. MAO-B inhibitors as monotherapy or adjunct
4. COMT inhibitors to extend levodopa effect
5. Deep brain stimulation for motor fluctuations
6. Physical therapy, speech therapy, occupational therapy
meds:
Carbidopa/levodopa 25/100mg TID (titrate to effect)
Pramipexole 0.125-1.5mg TID
Ropinirole 0.25-8mg TID
Rasagiline 1mg daily (MAO-B inhibitor)
Entacapone 200mg with each levodopa dose (COMT)
Amantadine 100mg BID (dyskinesias)
Rivastigmine patch 13.3mg/24h (dementia)
ddx: Essential tremor, MSA, PSP, Corticobasal degeneration, Lewy body dementia, Drug-induced parkinsonism, Normal pressure hydrocephalus, Wilson disease
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (no definitive test)
DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT) - confirms dopaminergic deficit
Brain MRI (rule out structural causes)
Copper/ceruloplasmin (Wilson disease if young)
TSH
Response to levodopa helps confirm diagnosis
notes:
Cardinal features: TRAP - Tremor, Rigidity, Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability
Asymmetric onset is classic (unilateral then bilateral)
Honeymoon period: good levodopa response for ~5 years
Motor complications: wearing off, on-off fluctuations, dyskinesias
Non-motor: depression, anxiety, constipation, orthostatic hypotension, RBD, cognitive decline
description: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the brain, leading to tremor, stiffness, and difficulty with movement.

name: Alzheimer's Disease
signs:
Progressive memory loss (short-term first)
Difficulty with familiar tasks
Language problems (word-finding difficulty)
Disorientation to time and place
Poor judgment
Personality and behavioral changes
Withdrawal from activities
Sundowning (evening agitation)
treatment:
1. Cholinesterase inhibitors for mild-moderate disease
2. Memantine for moderate-severe disease
3. Cognitive stimulation and engagement
4. Caregiver education and support
5. Safety planning (driving, wandering, finances)
6. Treat behavioral symptoms (depression, agitation, sleep)
7. Advance care planning early in disease
meds:
Donepezil 5-10mg PO daily
Rivastigmine patch 4.6-13.3mg/24h
Galantamine 4-12mg PO BID
Memantine 5-10mg PO BID (moderate-severe)
SSRI for depression/anxiety (sertraline 25-100mg)
Melatonin 3-6mg for sleep
Avoid: anticholinergics, benzodiazepines
ddx: Vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, Frontotemporal dementia, Normal pressure hydrocephalus, B12 deficiency, Hypothyroidism, Depression (pseudodementia), Medication side effects
labs:
Clinical diagnosis + cognitive testing (MMSE, MoCA)
Brain MRI (hippocampal/medial temporal atrophy, rule out other causes)
Amyloid PET scan
CSF biomarkers (low Abeta-42, elevated tau, p-tau)
CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, folate, RPR
Urinalysis (delirium workup)
Neuropsychological testing
notes:
Most common cause of dementia (60-80% of cases)
Pathology: amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles
Genetics: APOE4 allele increases risk; early-onset familial: APP, PSEN1, PSEN2 mutations
Staging: preclinical → MCI due to AD → mild → moderate → severe dementia
Medication side effects: avoid anticholinergics (Beers criteria)
Lecanemab: anti-amyloid antibody (modest benefit, ARIA risk)
description: The most common form of dementia, a progressive brain disorder that slowly destroys memory, thinking skills, and eventually the ability to carry out simple tasks.

name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
signs:
Sudden severe headache (thunderclap, worst of life)
Neck stiffness (meningismus)
Nausea/vomiting
Photophobia
Altered consciousness
Focal neurological deficits
Seizures
Subhyaloid (preretinal) hemorrhages
treatment:
1. Emergent neurosurgical consultation
2. Secure aneurysm: endovascular coiling (preferred) or surgical clipping
3. Nimodipine to prevent vasospasm
4. Blood pressure control (SBP <160 before securing)
5. EVD for hydrocephalus
6. Seizure prophylaxis (levetiracetam)
7. Prevent rebleeding: bedrest, stool softeners, analgesia
meds:
Nimodipine 60mg PO/NG q4h x21 days
Levetiracetam 500-1000mg IV BID
Labetalol IV for BP control
Fentanyl 25-50mcg IV PRN (pain)
Docusate 100mg BID (prevent straining)
3% hypertonic saline or mannitol (elevated ICP)
Aminocaproic acid (temporize if delay to surgery)
ddx: Migraine, Tension headache, Meningitis, Intracerebral hemorrhage, Cervical artery dissection, Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome
labs:
Non-contrast CT head (sensitivity 95% within 6h, decreases over time)
Lumbar puncture if CT negative (xanthochromia, elevated RBCs that don't clear)
CT angiography (identify aneurysm)
Conventional angiography (gold standard for aneurysm detection)
CBC, BMP, coags, type and screen
ECG (may show ST changes, QT prolongation)
notes:
Hunt-Hess grading: 1-mild headache, 2-severe headache/nuchal rigidity, 3-drowsy/focal deficit, 4-stupor, 5-coma
Fisher scale (CT blood): predicts vasospasm risk
Vasospasm peaks day 4-14: monitor with TCD, treat with triple-H or endovascular
Aneurysm locations: AComm (30%), PComm (25%), MCA (20%)
Rebleeding risk highest in first 24h - secure aneurysm ASAP
description: Bleeding into the space between the brain and its surrounding membrane, usually from a ruptured brain aneurysm, causing a sudden, severe headache and potential brain damage.

name: Migraine
signs:
Unilateral throbbing/pulsating headache
Nausea/vomiting
Photophobia and phonophobia
Aura (visual: scintillating scotoma, zigzag lines)
Duration 4-72 hours
Worsened by physical activity
Prodromes: yawning, food cravings, mood changes
treatment:
1. Acute: triptans (first-line for moderate-severe)
2. NSAIDs for mild-moderate attacks
3. Antiemetics for nausea
4. Preventive therapy if >=4 headache days/month
5. Identify and avoid triggers
6. CGRP monoclonal antibodies for prevention
7. Neuromodulation devices (gammaCore, SpringTMS)
meds:
Sumatriptan 50-100mg PO or 6mg SC (acute)
Rizatriptan 10mg PO
Ibuprofen 400-800mg PO
Metoclopramide 10mg IV (antiemetic + adjuvant)
Topiramate 25-100mg BID (prevention)
Propranolol 40-120mg BID (prevention)
Erenumab 70-140mg SC monthly (CGRP)
Botulinum toxin 155-195 units (chronic migraine)
ddx: Tension-type headache, Cluster headache, Subarachnoid hemorrhage, Meningitis, Temporal arteritis, Intracranial mass, Sinusitis
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (ICHD-3 criteria)
Brain MRI if: new onset >50yo, thunderclap, neurologic deficits, change in pattern
No routine labs needed
ESR if >50yo (temporal arteritis)
CT head to rule out hemorrhage if thunderclap onset
notes:
ICHD-3: >=5 attacks, 4-72h, >=2 of (unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, aggravated by activity), >=1 of (nausea/vomiting, photo+phonophobia)
With aura: visual most common, typically precedes headache by 5-60min
Chronic migraine: >=15 headache days/month for >=3 months
Medication overuse headache: triptans >10d/mo, analgesics >15d/mo
Triptans contraindicated in hemiplegic migraine, basilar migraine, uncontrolled HTN, CAD
description: A neurological condition causing recurring episodes of moderate to severe headache, often with nausea, light sensitivity, and sometimes visual disturbances called aura.

name: Transient Ischemic Attack
signs:
Sudden onset focal neurological deficit
Speech difficulty (aphasia, dysarthria)
Unilateral weakness or numbness
Visual loss (monocular = amaurosis fugax)
Ataxia
Symptoms resolve completely within 24h (usually <1h)
No evidence of infarction on imaging
treatment:
1. Emergent evaluation (treat as stroke until proven otherwise)
2. Antiplatelet therapy immediately
3. Carotid imaging (duplex US or CTA)
4. Cardiac monitoring (AFib detection)
5. Statin initiation
6. Blood pressure management
7. Carotid endarterectomy if >=70% ipsilateral stenosis
8. ABCD2 score for risk stratification
meds:
Aspirin 325mg PO immediately, then 81mg daily
Clopidogrel 75mg daily (dual antiplatelet x21 days per POINT trial)
Atorvastatin 80mg daily
Antihypertensive (after acute phase): lisinopril, amlodipine
Anticoagulation if AFib detected (apixaban 5mg BID)
ddx: Ischemic stroke, Migraine with aura, Seizure (Todd paralysis), Hypoglycemia, Conversion disorder, MS relapse, Brain tumor, Vestibular disorder
labs:
Brain MRI with DWI (rule out acute infarction)
CT head (rule out hemorrhage)
CTA head/neck or carotid duplex
ECG and cardiac monitoring (>=24h)
Echo (TTE or TEE)
CBC, BMP, glucose, lipids, HbA1c
PT/INR, PTT
ABCD2 score
notes:
ABCD2 score: Age >=60(1), BP >=140/90(1), Clinical (unilateral weakness 2, speech only 1), Duration (>=60min 2, 10-59min 1), Diabetes(1)
Score >=4: high 2-day stroke risk, consider hospitalization
Dual antiplatelet (ASA + clopidogrel) x21 days reduces 90-day stroke risk (CHANCE, POINT trials)
10-15% stroke risk within 90 days after TIA without treatment
Time-based definition replaced by tissue-based: TIA = transient symptoms + no infarction on DWI
description: A temporary episode of neurological dysfunction caused by brief interruption of blood flow to the brain, often called a 'mini-stroke,' serving as a warning sign for future stroke.

name: Epilepsy
signs:
Recurrent unprovoked seizures
Generalized tonic-clonic: loss of consciousness, stiffening, jerking
Absence: brief staring spells
Focal: motor, sensory, or autonomic symptoms
Post-ictal confusion
Tongue biting (lateral)
Urinary incontinence
Aura (focal onset)
treatment:
1. Anti-seizure medication (ASM) after 2 unprovoked seizures or 1 seizure with high recurrence risk
2. Choose ASM based on seizure type
3. Monotherapy preferred initially
4. Epilepsy surgery evaluation for drug-resistant epilepsy (failed >=2 ASMs)
5. Vagus nerve stimulation or responsive neurostimulation
6. Seizure precautions: driving restrictions, water safety
meds:
Levetiracetam 500-1500mg BID (broad-spectrum, first-line)
Lamotrigine 100-200mg BID (focal and generalized)
Valproate 500-1000mg BID (generalized - avoid in women of childbearing age)
Carbamazepine 200-600mg BID (focal only)
Oxcarbazepine 300-1200mg BID
Lacosamide 100-200mg BID
Brivaracetam 50-100mg BID
ddx: Syncope, Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES), TIA, Migraine, Hypoglycemia, Cardiac arrhythmia, Panic attack, Movement disorder
labs:
EEG (interictal epileptiform discharges)
Brain MRI (structural cause)
Prolactin level (elevated 10-20min post-seizure, helps differentiate from PNES)
BMP (electrolytes, glucose)
CBC
LFTs, drug levels for monitoring
Video-EEG monitoring (pre-surgical evaluation)
Genetic testing in select cases
notes:
Definition: >=2 unprovoked seizures >24h apart, OR 1 seizure + high recurrence risk (>=60%), OR epilepsy syndrome
Drug-resistant epilepsy: failed adequate trials of 2 ASMs
SUDEP (sudden unexpected death): risk ~1/1000 patient-years, higher with uncontrolled GTC seizures
Women: folic acid 1-4mg daily, lamotrigine/levetiracetam preferred in pregnancy
Driving: most states require 3-12 months seizure-free
description: A brain disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain, requiring long-term management with anti-seizure medications.

name: Bell's Palsy
signs:
Sudden unilateral facial weakness (upper AND lower face)
Inability to close eye on affected side
Drooping corner of mouth
Loss of nasolabial fold
Difficulty eating/drinking
Hyperacusis
Decreased taste (anterior 2/3 tongue)
Postauricular pain
treatment:
1. Oral corticosteroids within 72h of onset (most important)
2. Eye protection: artificial tears, eye patch at night
3. Antiviral added in severe cases (debated)
4. Physical therapy for facial exercises
5. Surgical decompression rarely needed
meds:
Prednisone 60-80mg daily x7 days then taper over 7 days
Valacyclovir 1g TID x7 days (severe/complete paralysis)
Artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose) q1-2h while awake
Lacri-Lube ointment at night
Taping eyelid shut at night
ddx: Stroke (central facial palsy - spares forehead), Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster + vesicles), Lyme disease, Otitis media, Parotid tumor, Sarcoidosis, GBS, Cholesteatoma
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
Lyme serology if endemic area or bilateral
MRI brain/IAC if atypical or no improvement in 3 weeks
Electroneurography (ENoG) if complete paralysis
No routine labs needed for typical presentation
HIV testing if risk factors
notes:
Upper vs lower motor neuron: Bell's palsy = LMN (entire half of face); stroke = UMN (spares forehead)
Recovery: 85% recover completely within 3 months
House-Brackmann grading scale (I-VI)
Ramsay Hunt: facial palsy + vesicular eruption on ear + hearing loss (worse prognosis)
Bilateral facial palsy: think Lyme, GBS, sarcoidosis, HIV
Incomplete eye closure: risk of corneal ulceration
description: Sudden weakness or paralysis of the muscles on one side of the face due to inflammation of the facial nerve, usually temporary and thought to be triggered by viral infections.

name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
signs:
Severe, electric shock-like facial pain
Unilateral, along trigeminal nerve distribution (V2/V3 most common)
Brief paroxysms (seconds to 2 minutes)
Trigger zones (touching face, chewing, talking, brushing teeth)
Pain-free intervals between attacks
No neurological deficit on exam
treatment:
1. Carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine (first-line)
2. Add baclofen or lamotrigine if partial response
3. Microvascular decompression (surgery for refractory)
4. Gamma knife radiosurgery
5. Percutaneous procedures (balloon compression, glycerol rhizolysis)
6. MRI to rule out secondary causes
meds:
Carbamazepine 100-400mg BID (first-line)
Oxcarbazepine 150-600mg BID
Baclofen 10-20mg TID (adjunct)
Lamotrigine 200-400mg daily
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (less effective)
Phenytoin 200-400mg daily (acute IV loading option)
ddx: Dental pathology, TMJ disorder, Cluster headache, Post-herpetic neuralgia, MS (bilateral TN in young patient), Glossopharyngeal neuralgia, Sinusitis, Tumor
labs:
Brain MRI with thin cuts through posterior fossa (rule out tumor, MS, vascular loop)
MRA (identify vascular compression)
No routine labs needed
Dental evaluation to rule out odontogenic pain
Carbamazepine: check HLA-B*1502 in Asian descent (SJS risk), monitor CBC and sodium
notes:
Classic TN: caused by neurovascular compression (usually SCA)
Secondary TN: caused by MS, tumor, AVM
V2 (maxillary) and V3 (mandibular) most commonly affected
Bilateral TN: consider MS (especially in young patients)
Carbamazepine: monitor CBC (agranulocytosis), sodium (hyponatremia), LFTs
Microvascular decompression: 80% long-term pain relief
description: A chronic pain condition affecting the trigeminal nerve in the face, causing sudden, severe, shock-like pain episodes typically triggered by everyday activities like eating or touching the face.

name: Cluster Headache
signs:
Severe unilateral periorbital/temporal pain
Autonomic features: lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea
Partial Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis)
Restlessness/agitation during attack
Attacks 15-180 minutes
Circadian pattern (often wake from sleep)
1-8 attacks per day during cluster period
treatment:
1. Acute: high-flow oxygen 12-15L/min via NRB x15-20min
2. Sumatriptan SC for acute attacks
3. Transitional: prednisone burst, occipital nerve block
4. Preventive: verapamil (first-line)
5. Galcanezumab for episodic cluster headache
6. Avoid alcohol during cluster period
meds:
Oxygen 100% 12-15L/min via NRB (acute, first-line)
Sumatriptan 6mg SC (acute)
Zolmitriptan 5mg nasal spray (acute)
Verapamil 240-960mg daily in divided doses (prevention)
Prednisone 60mg daily x5 days then taper (transitional)
Lithium 300mg BID-TID (prevention)
Galcanezumab 300mg SC monthly x3
ddx: Migraine, Trigeminal neuralgia, Paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA, Temporal arteritis, Pheochromocytoma, Carotid dissection
labs:
Clinical diagnosis
Brain MRI (rule out secondary causes: pituitary tumor, cavernous sinus lesion)
ESR if >50yo (temporal arteritis)
ECG before verapamil
Lithium level, TSH, BMP if on lithium
PSG if sleep apnea suspected (comorbid)
notes:
Male predominance 3:1, onset 20-40 years
Episodic: cluster periods 2 weeks-3 months with remission >=3 months
Chronic: no remission >3 months (10-15%)
Verapamil: get ECG at baseline and with dose increases (heart block risk)
Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia (TAC) family
Indometacin-responsive: paroxysmal hemicrania (not cluster)
Key difference from migraine: restlessness vs. wanting to lie still
description: An extremely painful headache disorder that occurs in cyclical patterns (clusters), causing severe one-sided head pain with eye watering and nasal congestion.

name: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
signs:
Gait apraxia (magnetic/shuffling gait - earliest and most responsive to treatment)
Urinary incontinence
Dementia (subcortical pattern)
Classic triad: wet, wacky, wobbly
Symptoms develop over months
Ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy
treatment:
1. High-volume lumbar puncture (30-50mL CSF removal) as diagnostic/therapeutic trial
2. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt placement (definitive)
3. Endoscopic third ventriculostomy (select cases)
4. Physical therapy for gait rehabilitation
5. Cognitive rehabilitation
6. Shunt monitoring and adjustment
meds:
No primary pharmacologic treatment
Acetazolamide 250-500mg BID (temporizing, limited evidence)
Treat comorbid conditions
Avoid anticholinergics (worsen cognition)
Manage UTI if incontinence-related infections
ddx: Alzheimer's disease, Vascular dementia, Parkinson's disease, Cervical spondylotic myelopathy, B12 deficiency, Depression, Urological causes of incontinence
labs:
Brain MRI (ventriculomegaly, Evans index >0.3, DESH pattern, tight high convexity)
High-volume LP (gait improvement after removing 30-50mL CSF = positive tap test)
CSF pressure (normal or high-normal: <18cmH2O)
Continuous lumbar drainage trial (72h)
Cisternography (less used)
Neuropsychological testing
Timed Up and Go test (before and after LP)
notes:
Triad: gait disturbance (earliest, most responsive to shunt) → urinary incontinence → dementia
Evans index = max width of frontal horns / max internal skull diameter (>0.3 = ventriculomegaly)
Best shunt candidates: known cause (SAH, meningitis), short symptom duration, gait predominant
Shunt complications: subdural hematoma, infection, over-drainage, obstruction
Gait improvement post-LP predicts shunt response (80% positive predictive value)
description: A brain condition where cerebrospinal fluid accumulates in the ventricles despite normal pressure readings, causing the classic triad of walking difficulty, urinary incontinence, and memory problems.

name: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
signs:
Progressive muscle weakness (asymmetric)
Muscle atrophy and fasciculations (LMN signs)
Hyperreflexia and spasticity (UMN signs)
Dysarthria and dysphagia (bulbar onset)
No sensory loss
Emotional lability (pseudobulbar affect)
Respiratory failure (late)
treatment:
1. Riluzole (modest survival benefit)
2. Edaravone (may slow functional decline)
3. Multidisciplinary care (neurology, pulm, PT/OT/SLP, nutrition, palliative)
4. BiPAP for respiratory support
5. PEG tube for nutrition when dysphagia progresses
6. Communication devices
7. Palliative care and advance directives early
meds:
Riluzole 50mg PO BID
Edaravone 60mg IV daily x14 days in 28-day cycles
Tofersen (SOD1 mutation only)
Dextromethorphan/quinidine 20/10mg BID (pseudobulbar affect)
Baclofen 10-20mg TID (spasticity)
Lorazepam 0.5-1mg SL (end-of-life dyspnea)
Glycopyrrolate 1-2mg TID (sialorrhea)
ddx: Cervical myelopathy, Multifocal motor neuropathy, Myasthenia gravis, Kennedy disease, Inclusion body myositis, Primary lateral sclerosis, MS
labs:
EMG/NCV (active and chronic denervation in multiple regions, normal sensory)
Brain and spine MRI (rule out structural causes)
CBC, CMP, TSH, B12
Creatine kinase (mildly elevated)
PFTs with MIP/MEP (respiratory monitoring)
Genetic testing (SOD1, C9orf72 if familial)
Anti-GM1 antibodies (rule out MMN)
notes:
El Escorial criteria: UMN + LMN signs in multiple regions, progressive spread, no alternative explanation
Median survival 3-5 years from symptom onset
Bulbar onset: worse prognosis (2-3 years)
FVC <50% predicted: consider BiPAP
5-10% familial; C9orf72 repeat expansion most common genetic cause
Sensory involvement EXCLUDES ALS - look for alternative diagnosis
description: A progressive neurodegenerative disease that destroys motor neurons controlling voluntary muscles, leading to increasing paralysis while typically leaving mental function intact.

name: Encephalitis
signs:
Altered mental status
Fever
Seizures
Focal neurological deficits
Headache
Behavioral/personality changes
Movement disorders
Language dysfunction
Memory impairment (temporal lobe involvement)
treatment:
1. Empiric acyclovir immediately (do not wait for results)
2. Supportive care: airway, seizure management, ICP monitoring
3. Dexamethasone for autoimmune encephalitis
4. Immunotherapy for autoimmune: IVIG, plasma exchange, rituximab
5. Seizure prophylaxis
6. ICU care for severe cases
meds:
Acyclovir 10mg/kg IV q8h x14-21 days (HSV empiric)
Levetiracetam 500-1500mg BID (seizure prophylaxis)
Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg IV q6h (bacterial/autoimmune)
IVIG 0.4g/kg/day x5 days (autoimmune)
Rituximab 375mg/m2 weekly x4 (refractory autoimmune)
Mannitol 1g/kg IV (elevated ICP)
ddx: Meningitis, Brain abscess, Status epilepticus, Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR), Metabolic encephalopathy, Toxic ingestion, CNS vasculitis, Prion disease
labs:
Brain MRI (temporal lobe involvement suggests HSV)
CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose, HSV PCR
EEG (PLEDs in HSV, extreme delta brush in anti-NMDAR)
CSF viral panel (HSV, VZV, enterovirus, arbovirus)
Autoimmune antibodies: anti-NMDAR, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2
HIV test
Blood cultures
CBC, BMP, LFTs
notes:
HSV encephalitis: most common sporadic cause, temporal lobe predilection, mortality >70% untreated
Start acyclovir empirically in ALL suspected encephalitis - do not delay for LP/MRI
Anti-NMDAR encephalitis: young women, psychiatric symptoms, movement disorder, autonomic instability (check for ovarian teratoma)
Arboviral: West Nile, Eastern equine, Japanese encephalitis (geographic/seasonal)
Autoimmune encephalitis increasingly recognized - check antibody panels
description: Inflammation of the brain itself, most commonly caused by viral infections like herpes simplex, leading to altered consciousness, seizures, and potentially permanent brain damage if not treated promptly.

name: Spinal Cord Compression
signs:
Progressive weakness below level of compression
Sensory level (band-like sensation at level)
Urinary retention or incontinence
Bowel dysfunction
Back pain (localized)
Upper motor neuron signs below lesion (hyperreflexia, Babinski)
Saddle anesthesia (conus/cauda equina)
treatment:
1. Emergent MRI of entire spine
2. IV dexamethasone if malignant (10mg bolus then 4mg q6h)
3. Urgent surgical decompression
4. Radiation therapy for malignant compression
5. Bladder catheterization
6. DVT prophylaxis
7. Rehabilitation after stabilization
meds:
Dexamethasone 10mg IV bolus then 4mg IV q6h (malignant)
Opioid analgesics for pain
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (neuropathic pain)
Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (DVT prophylaxis)
Bisacodyl/docusate (bowel program)
Baclofen 10-20mg TID (spasticity)
ddx: Transverse myelitis, MS, Epidural abscess, Spinal cord infarction, GBS, Cauda equina syndrome, Vitamin B12 deficiency, Spinal AVM
labs:
MRI spine with contrast (urgently - entire spine)
CT myelography if MRI contraindicated
Plain radiographs (fracture, metastatic disease)
CBC, ESR, CRP (infection/malignancy)
PSA, SPEP (malignancy workup)
UA and post-void residual (bladder function)
Blood cultures if epidural abscess suspected
notes:
Surgical emergency - time to intervention determines outcome
Most common cause: metastatic disease (breast, lung, prostate, renal, myeloma)
Anterior cord syndrome: motor + pain/temp loss, preserved proprioception
Central cord syndrome: upper > lower extremity weakness, common in cervical hyperextension
Brown-Sequard: ipsilateral motor + proprioception loss, contralateral pain/temp loss
Bladder dysfunction often indicates poor prognosis for recovery
description: Pressure on the spinal cord from tumors, fractures, or abscesses that can cause progressive weakness, numbness, and loss of bladder/bowel control, requiring urgent treatment to prevent permanent paralysis.

name: Essential Tremor
signs:
Bilateral action/postural tremor (hands most common)
Frequency 4-12 Hz
Improves with alcohol
Head tremor (titubation)
Voice tremor
No resting tremor component
Absence of other neurological findings
Family history common
Worsens with stress, fatigue, caffeine
treatment:
1. Reassurance if mild and not functionally limiting
2. Propranolol or primidone (first-line)
3. Topiramate or gabapentin (second-line)
4. Botulinum toxin for head/voice tremor
5. Deep brain stimulation (VIM thalamus) for refractory disabling tremor
6. MRI-guided focused ultrasound thalamotomy
meds:
Propranolol 60-320mg daily (long-acting preferred)
Primidone 25mg at bedtime, titrate to 250mg TID
Topiramate 25-200mg BID
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID
Alprazolam 0.25-0.5mg TID (short-term, situational)
Botulinum toxin injections (head/voice tremor)
ddx: Parkinson's disease (resting tremor), Physiologic tremor (enhanced), Cerebellar tremor, Drug-induced tremor, Hyperthyroidism, Wilson disease, Dystonic tremor
labs:
Clinical diagnosis - no definitive test
TSH (rule out hyperthyroidism)
Ceruloplasmin, copper (Wilson disease if <40yo)
DaTscan (to differentiate from Parkinson's if unclear)
Brain MRI (rule out structural cause)
LFTs if starting primidone
notes:
Most common movement disorder (estimated 4-5% of population)
Autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance
Key differentiator from PD: action/postural (ET) vs resting tremor (PD), bilateral (ET) vs asymmetric (PD)
Alcohol responsiveness supports diagnosis but don't recommend as treatment
DBS of VIM nucleus: 70-90% tremor reduction
Often misdiagnosed as Parkinson's disease
description: The most common movement disorder, causing involuntary rhythmic shaking primarily of the hands during voluntary movements, often running in families and typically worsening slowly over time.
